text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY SURVIVORSHIP CLINIC
OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE — 18-MONTH POST-TREATMENT SURVEILLANCE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Head and Neck Medical Oncology
DEPARTMENT: Head and Neck Oncology / Survivorship

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 53-year-old female with a history of HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue (T2N1M0, p16-positive, HPV-16), who presents today for her 18-month post-treatment surveillance visit. She is seen in the Head and Neck Oncology Survivorship Clinic for ongoing monitoring following completion of definitive concurrent chemoradiation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 53-year-old never-smoking West African woman and high school music teacher who was diagnosed approximately 18 months ago with p16-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue (T2N1M0, confirmed HPV-16 by RNA ISH), with a single right level IIA metastatic lymph node (2.1 cm, necrotic). She was treated definitively with concurrent intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT using volumetric modulated arc therapy) to 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-risk CTV, with simultaneous integrated boost, completed over 7 weeks, plus weekly cis 40 mg/m2 IV (6 of 7 planned cycles delivered; week 5 held due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity and thrombocytopenia; cumulative dose ~414 mg/m2).

Her treatment course was complicated by grade 3 oral mucositis requiring a 3-day hospital admission during week 4 of radiation, initiation of PEG tube feeds, and IV hydration. She also experienced grade 2 radiation dermatitis, grade 2 dysgeusia, grade 2 xerostomia, and transient grade 2 nephrotoxicity during treatment. She achieved complete metabolic response on restaging PET-CT at 10 weeks post-treatment, and confirmed complete pathologic response on direct laryngoscopy with biopsies at approximately 5 months post-treatment (no viable tumor identified; biopsies showed radiation-effect changes with fibrosis only). PEG tube was removed at approximately 7 months post-treatment after she demonstrated adequate oral intake. At the 12-month annual survivorship visit she reported return to work as a music teacher, weight recovery to 71 kg, and no clinical evidence of disease on physical examination or flexible nasopharyngoscopy.

She presents today at the 18-month mark for her scheduled surveillance evaluation. She is accompanied by her partner.

Interval history: She reports continued gradual improvement in function and quality of life since her last visit 6 months ago. She reports no new neck masses, no new oral lesions, no dysphagia progression, no odynophagia, no otalgia, no trismus worsening, no hoarseness change, and no constitutional symptoms (no fevers, no chills, no unintentional weight loss, no night sweats). She is pleased with her functional recovery.

Swallowing: She is now tolerating a regular diet with only occasional need to modify texture for particularly dry or sticky foods (e.g., dry bread, crackers, thick peanut butter). She reports no choking or aspiration episodes. She no longer uses the PEG tube, which was removed several months ago, and the PEG site has fully healed. She has made no special diet adjustments for work lunches. She estimates she is eating approximately 85-90% of her usual pre-treatment diet by variety.

Xerostomia: She reports persistent dry mouth, which she describes as moderately bothersome but improved compared to earlier in her recovery. She sleeps with a water bottle at bedside and sips frequently during the day. She continues Biotene oral moisturizing gel and mouthwash. She is on pilocarpine (which was recently reduced to 5 mg twice daily from three times daily), which she states does provide modest benefit, particularly for meals. She reports that the dry mouth interferes somewhat with singing and sustained vocal projection in class, which is professionally relevant given her role as a music teacher.

Voice: Mild hoarseness noted throughout recovery; she reports this is stable and she has adapted her teaching style. She is not using amplification at work at this time.

Trismus/jaw: She has been doing daily jaw stretching exercises with the TheraBite device consistently since her last visit. She reports improved range of motion. No significant pain with jaw opening. No difficulty with dental hygiene.

Hearing: She has noticed some high-frequency hearing loss, particularly when in noisy environments (e.g., school gymnasium, cafeteria). She has been counseled that this is related to cis exposure and appears stable on audiometry. She is not using hearing aids currently.

Thyroid: She is on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily following dose increase at 6 months post-treatment for radiation-induced hypothyroidism. She reports no symptoms of hypothyroidism (no fatigue beyond baseline, no constipation, no cold intolerance, no weight gain beyond expected recovery).

Psychosocial: She reports that her mood has continued to improve. She returned to full-time work at 10 months post-treatment and states that reconnecting with her students and the routine of teaching has been psychologically restorative. She is aware of the significance of reaching the 18-month disease-free milestone and expresses cautious optimism. She has a strong support network. No clinical depression or anxiety symptoms endorsed today. She has been speaking with the oncology social worker intermittently.

Medications (current):
- Levothyroxine 100 mcg PO daily (radiation-induced hypothyroidism)
- Pilocarpine 5 mg PO BID (xerostomia)
- Biotene mouthwash and oral gel (as needed)
- Saline nasal spray (as needed for post-radiation nasal dryness)
- Multivitamin daily
- Atorvastatin 10 mg PO daily (initiated 30 months post-treatment for cardiovascular risk reduction given carotid radiation exposure — NOTE: this entry reflects prospective medication as of 30-month visit, not this 18-month visit)

Current medications as of this 18-month visit:
- Levothyroxine 100 mcg PO daily
- Pilocarpine 5 mg PO BID
- Biotene gel and mouthwash PRN
- Saline nasal spray PRN
- Multivitamin daily

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: No fever, no chills, no unintentional weight loss, no drenching night sweats. Weight stable to slightly improved since last visit.
HEENT: Persistent grade 1-2 xerostomia. Stable mild hoarseness. No new oral lesions. No dysphagia beyond chronic mild post-treatment limitation. No otalgia. No new neck masses. No epistaxis. No facial numbness.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no exertional dyspnea.
Respiratory: No cough, no hemoptysis, no shortness of breath.
Gastrointestinal: Tolerating regular oral diet. No nausea, no vomiting. No abdominal pain. Bowel habits normal. PEG site healed.
Genitourinary: No hematuria. No dysuria. Menses irregular (peri-menopausal; noted at prior visits, not directly treatment-related).
Musculoskeletal: No new musculoskeletal complaints. No neck stiffness. Jaw mobility improving.
Neurological: No headaches. No vision changes. No focal neurological symptoms. Mild subjective high-frequency hearing loss, stable.
Endocrine: On levothyroxine for radiation-induced hypothyroidism, well-controlled per recent TSH.
Psychiatric: Mood improved, no active depression or anxiety endorsed. Sleeping well.
Dermatologic: Right neck radiation field: residual hyperpigmentation and mild skin texture change. No active dermatitis. PEG site fully healed.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished female in no acute distress. Appears her stated age. Alert and oriented. Pleasant and communicative.

Vital signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16, temperature 37.0°C, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Weight: 70 kg (up approximately 1 kg from last visit; still approximately 7 kg below pre-treatment baseline of 77 kg). BMI: 24.2 kg/m2.

Head: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No facial asymmetry.

Eyes: PERRL bilaterally, no icterus, no scleral injection.

Oral cavity / oropharynx: Mouth opening approximately 4.1 cm (improved from 3.8 cm at 12-month visit and baseline trismus estimate of 4.5 cm pre-treatment). Oral mucosa: mildly dry with reduced salivary pooling, consistent with xerostomia, no active mucositis, no ulceration, no leukoplakia, no erythema suspicious for recurrence. Tongue: mobile, no masses. Dentition intact; no periapical disease noted on visual inspection. Hard and soft palate intact, no lesions. Oropharynx: post-radiation mucosal changes at the right base of tongue region with smooth, pale, fibrotic-appearing mucosa; no exophytic mass, no ulceration, no neovascularization or friability to suggest recurrent tumor. Residual right tonsillar fossa (post-biopsy/post-radiation): smooth, fibrotic, no visible lesion.

Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally. No palpable masses in the right or left neck. Right neck skin: residual post-radiation hyperpigmentation and mild subcutaneous textural change in the treatment field; no active dermatitis; no lymphedema; no skin breakdown. Thyroid: not enlarged, no nodules palpable. No tracheal deviation.

Flexible nasopharyngoscopy (performed in clinic today): Nasal passages patent bilaterally, no mucosal lesions. Nasopharynx: smooth mucosa, no masses. Soft palate: symmetrical elevation. Oropharynx: right base of tongue with stable post-radiation fibrotic changes; smooth, pale mucosa without any mucosal irregularity, exophytic component, or friability; the tongue base appears symmetrical; no visible lesion. Larynx: bilateral true vocal cords appear mobile and symmetrical with mild mucosal irregularity of the right true vocal cord consistent with post-radiation changes; no visible lesion or mass. Epiglottis: smooth, no mass. Hypopharynx: no pooling of secretions, no lesions. Piriform sinuses: patent bilaterally, no masses.

Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezing, no crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. PEG site fully healed with residual small scar on abdominal wall; no induration or drainage. Bowel sounds present.
Extremities: No edema. No clubbing. No cyanosis.
Neurological: CN II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits.

---

LAB RESULTS

Comprehensive metabolic panel (recent, obtained prior to today's visit):
- Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride 103 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L
- BUN 16 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (at pre-treatment baseline; no evidence of persistent nephrotoxicity)
- Glucose 88 mg/dL
- ALT 22 U/L, AST 19 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 71 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.5 mg/dL
- Albumin 4.2 g/dL

Complete blood count:
- WBC 6.1 x10^9/L with normal differential
- Hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable, likely chronic)
- Platelets 241 x10^9/L

Thyroid function:
- TSH: 1.8 mIU/L (normal range 0.4-4.0 mIU/L) — well-controlled hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily; normalized compared to TSH 6.8 at 6-month visit and TSH 2.1 at 12-month visit.
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)

Magnesium: 2.0 mg/dL (low-normal; historically has had mild hypomagnesemia related to Platinol; will monitor).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

MRI neck with and without contrast (18-month surveillance imaging, obtained prior to today's visit):

The MRI shows stable to improving post-treatment changes at the right base of tongue. The previously noted 0.9 cm area of fibrosis that was identified on the 10-week post-treatment MRI has continued to decrease in size, now measuring approximately 0.5 cm, and demonstrates no enhancement on post-contrast sequences — this is reassuring and consistent with maturing post-radiation fibrosis rather than residual or recurrent tumor. There is no new mucosal thickening or submucosal mass at the primary site. The right level IIA lymph node is stable at 0.7 cm with a fibrotic appearance and no internal necrosis or pathologic enhancement. No new adenopathy is identified elsewhere in the neck. No new lesions. The overall impression from radiology was no radiographic evidence of recurrent disease; stable and improving post-treatment fibrotic changes.

This is consistent with an ongoing complete response and is highly reassuring at the 18-month timepoint.

Audiogram (obtained at this visit by audiology, 18-month post-Platinol):
High-frequency sensorineural hearing loss bilaterally, predominantly at 4000-8000 Hz. Grade 1 by CTCAE criteria. Stable compared to 12-month audiogram — no progression of cis-related ototoxicity. Air-bone gap absent; findings consistent with sensorineural pattern. Speech discrimination score 96% bilaterally. Patient does not meet criteria for hearing aid referral at this time; will continue annual monitoring.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-POSITIVE RIGHT BASE OF TONGUE SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (T2N1M0, p16-positive, HPV-16), STATUS POST DEFINITIVE CONCURRENT CHEMORADIATION — COMPLETE RESPONSE, NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE AT 18 MONTHS.

Ms. Osei-Bonsu continues to do extremely well at her 18-month surveillance visit. She has no clinical, endoscopic, or radiographic evidence of disease recurrence. The MRI of the neck today is reassuring — the fibrotic area at the right base of tongue has continued to shrink and shows no enhancement, which is highly consistent with maturing post-radiation fibrosis rather than residual or recurrent tumor. The right level IIA node remains small, fibrotic, and unremarkable. The flexible nasopharyngoscopy performed in clinic today shows smooth, stable post-radiation fibrotic changes at the base of tongue with no mucosal irregularity, friability, or mass effect to suggest recurrence.

I discussed with Ms. Osei-Bonsu that reaching 18 months of disease-free status following definitive treatment for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC is a meaningful and encouraging milestone. The biology of HPV-positive OPSCC — in contrast to HPV-negative disease — is characterized by excellent response rates to chemoradiation and favorable long-term outcomes in never-smokers with complete response, such as herself. The 5-year locoregional control and overall survival rates for patients with her profile (T2N1M0, HPV-positive, never-smoker, complete response by both imaging and biopsy) are in the range of 85-90%.

Plan:
- Continue surveillance per modified NCCN schedule: clinical exam and flexible nasopharyngoscopy every 4-6 months, given she is now past the 18-month mark. Annual surveillance imaging with either MRI or PET-CT, timing of next dedicated surveillance imaging to be determined (likely at the 24-month mark).
- Will order PET-CT at 24 months (6 months from now) as the planned 2-year surveillance imaging study.
- No adjuvant or additional systemic therapy warranted. Complete pathologic and metabolic response has been confirmed.
- No biopsies needed at this time given the reassuring appearance on both MRI and nasopharyngoscopy.

2. LATE RADIATION TOXICITIES — XEROSTOMIA, DYSPHAGIA, TRISMUS

Xerostomia: Persistent grade 1-2. This is an expected late effect from bilateral neck irradiation, even with parotid-sparing IMRT (mean contralateral parotid dose was 22 Gy in her plan). She is managing adequately with pilocarpine 5 mg BID, Biotene, and sipping water. I will continue pilocarpine at 5 mg BID — the taper to BID from TID appears well-tolerated. She will continue Biotene oral products. I discussed that salivary gland recovery, while variable, can continue for 12-24 months post-treatment in some patients, and we remain hopeful for further modest improvement. Patient verbalized professional concern about voice projection and singing ability given her role as a music teacher; will refer to speech-language pathology for a focused session on voice hygiene and compensation strategies in the setting of xerostomia.

Dysphagia: She is tolerating a regular-modified diet and manages well. She continues to avoid dry and sticky foods. Speech-language pathology recommends quarterly sessions with continued home exercise program targeting base of tongue retraction and pharyngeal constriction; she will continue.

Trismus: Mouth opening improved to 4.1 cm from 3.8 cm at 12-month visit, which is meaningful progress and attributable to consistent TheraBite exercises. Encourage continued TheraBite use at least twice daily. Goal is to maintain and ideally continue progress toward 4.5 cm (estimated pre-treatment baseline).

3. RADIATION-INDUCED HYPOTHYROIDISM

TSH 1.8 mIU/L — well-controlled on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. No symptoms of hypothyroidism. Continue levothyroxine at current dose. Annual thyroid function monitoring. Annual thyroid ultrasound to screen for radiation-induced thyroid nodules/cancer is initiated starting at this visit (first screening ultrasound to be ordered).

Thyroid ultrasound: Ordered today — first screening ultrasound in the context of prior neck irradiation per NCCN guidelines for radiation-exposed thyroid gland. Results will be reviewed at next visit or earlier if abnormal.

4. cis-RELATED OTOTOXICITY

Stable grade 1 high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss bilaterally, unchanged from 12-month audiogram. No progression. Patient is not meeting criteria for hearing aids. Will continue annual audiometry. She should avoid additional ototoxic agents if at all possible in future medical care. She has been counseled on hearing protection in loud environments (e.g., school band room, gymnasium).

5. HYPOMAGNESEMIA (MILD, CHRONIC)

Magnesium 2.0 mg/dL — low-normal, attributable to ongoing mild Platinol-related renal tubular dysfunction. Clinically asymptomatic. Will initiate oral magnesium oxide supplementation 400 mg PO daily and recheck magnesium at next visit.

6. PSYCHOSOCIAL AND FUNCTIONAL STATUS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu continues to demonstrate excellent functional recovery. She is back to full-time work as a music teacher, which is a remarkable achievement given the severity of her acute toxicities during treatment. ECOG performance status is 0 at this visit. Mood and psychological adjustment are appropriate. No active distress. Will continue periodic contact with oncology social work as she transitions toward less frequent follow-up, which can be psychologically challenging for some patients ("surveillance anxiety").

7. GERMLINE BRCA2 VARIANT

Reminder: NGS at diagnosis identified a germline-concerning BRCA2 p.D2723H variant (AF 48%, likely germline). Genetics referral was previously placed. Patient was seen by genetics counseling and this variant is being evaluated. Will confirm status at next visit. No additional specific management planned pending reclassification.

FOLLOW-UP:
- Return in 4-6 months for next surveillance clinic visit (approximately 22-24 months post-treatment).
- PET-CT at 24 months post-treatment to be scheduled.
- Thyroid ultrasound ordered today, results to be reviewed.
- Oral magnesium supplementation initiated.
- SLP referral placed for voice/xerostomia management.
- Laboratory follow-up: repeat TSH in 6 months, repeat magnesium at next visit.
- Annual audiometry.
- Patient instructed to call clinic promptly if she develops any new neck mass, change in swallowing, new oral lesion, otalgia, unexplained weight loss, or any other concerning symptom.

Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes, including examination, review of imaging, and patient counseling.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Head and Neck Medical Oncology
